title: When Tourism Saves a Town — and When It Doesn't
summary: A British narrator describes how two similar coastal towns took very different paths with tourism, and what their experiences reveal about the limits of visitor-led growth.
body: Two small towns sit thirty miles apart on the same stretch of coast. Both were fishing ports until the 1980s, when the industry collapsed. Both lost half their population over the following decade, and both turned to tourism to survive. Today, one of them is thriving. The other is being eaten alive by its own success. The first town, which I'll call Greycombe, was lucky in the people who led the recovery. Local councillors decided that tourism would be useful only if it created year-round jobs and protected what made the town worth visiting in the first place. They limited new hotels to the seafront, refused short-term rental conversions in the old centre, and invested in a small but reliable winter programme of festivals and food markets. As a result, the population has stabilised, schools have stayed open, and most of the houses are still occupied by people who actually live there. The second town, which I'll call Marshpoint, took the opposite approach. Anyone who wanted to convert a fisherman's cottage into a short-term holiday let was welcomed. Within ten years, more than a third of the old centre had been bought up by people who only visit for three weeks a year. Locals who grew up there cannot afford to buy a house, and many have moved inland. The high street is full of fudge shops and surfboard rentals, but the bakery has closed, the chemist has closed, and the primary school is struggling for pupils. When you walk around Marshpoint in February, it feels like a stage set after the play has ended. What this tells us is not that tourism is bad. Greycombe has shown that the same industry can sustain a real community. The difference lies in two questions almost no town asks early enough. The first is: what kind of town do we want to be in twenty years? The second is: who actually lives here, and who is just here for the weekend? If the answer to the second question becomes 'mostly weekenders', the answer to the first question is being decided by people who do not bear the costs. By the time a town realises this, the cost of reversing it is usually higher than the cost of preventing it. There is no perfect rule for managing tourism, and copying Greycombe will not save Marshpoint. The economic conditions are different, the planning powers are different, and the political will is different. But the broad lesson holds: a town that lets tourism replace its other industries will eventually become a museum of itself, kept alive by visitors who would lose interest if the locals ever fully disappeared. Tourism can save a town. It can also hollow it out so quietly that no one notices until the school closes. The difference is in the rules a town sets while it still has the power to set them.

annotations.exampleEn: Three resorts share the same stretch of coast.
Most of the small fishing ports closed by the late 1980s.
When the industry collapsed, half the town moved away.

annotations.phrase: being eaten alive by its own success
annotations.meaningVi: đang bị chính thành công của mình ăn tươi nuốt sống
annotations.pronunciation: /ˈbiːɪŋ ˈiːtən əˈlaɪv baɪ ɪts əʊn səkˈses/
annotations.exampleEn: The festival is being eaten alive by its own success — it's lost its character.
We need year-round jobs, not just summer ones.

annotations.meaningVi: việc chuyển đổi nhà thành cho thuê ngắn hạn
annotations.exampleEn: The council banned short-term rental conversions in the old quarter.
The population has stabilised after twenty years of decline.
Most of the cottages have been bought up by outsiders.

annotations.meaningVi: đã chuyển vào sâu trong đất liền
annotations.exampleEn: Priced out of the village, many families moved inland.

annotations.meaningVi: phố chính (phố thương mại của thị trấn)
annotations.exampleEn: The high street is dominated by gift shops now.

annotations.meaningVi: cửa hàng kẹo fudge (thường thấy ở khu du lịch)
annotations.exampleEn: Every seaside town in England has at least three fudge shops.

annotations.meaningVi: phông cảnh sân khấu (trống rỗng sau buổi diễn)
annotations.exampleEn: In winter, the village feels like a stage set after the play has ended.
The people who decide are not the people who bear the costs.
Reversing it later costs far more than preventing it now.

annotations.meaningVi: bảo tàng của chính nó (chỉ còn vẻ ngoài, mất linh hồn)
annotations.exampleEn: The village has become a museum of itself, with no real life left.

annotations.meaningVi: làm rỗng ruột, làm cạn kiệt nội lực
annotations.exampleEn: Unchecked tourism can hollow a town out very quietly.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice for Process and Consequence
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng dạng bị động để mô tả quá trình ảnh hưởng đến cộng đồng: 'Anyone who wanted to convert a fisherman's cottage into a short-term holiday let was welcomed', 'more than a third of the old centre had been bought up', 'kept alive by visitors', 'is being decided by people who do not bear the costs'. Cấu trúc: be + V3. Dùng khi muốn nhấn mạnh hành động hoặc kết quả thay vì người thực hiện.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses passive voice to describe processes affecting the community: 'was welcomed', 'had been bought up', 'kept alive by visitors', 'is being decided by people who do not bear the costs'. Form: be + past participle. Use when the action or outcome matters more than who performs it.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Speculation and Conditional Predictions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng các động từ khuyết thiếu và câu điều kiện để dự đoán tương lai: 'a town that lets tourism replace its other industries will eventually become a museum', 'copying Greycombe will not save Marshpoint', 'who would lose interest if the locals ever fully disappeared'. 'Will' diễn tả dự đoán có cơ sở; loại 2 (would + V) diễn tả tình huống giả định.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses modals and conditionals to predict consequences: 'will eventually become', 'will not save', 'who would lose interest if the locals ever fully disappeared'. 'Will' expresses a confident prediction; second conditional (would + verb) describes a hypothetical scenario.

translationVi: Hai thị trấn nhỏ nằm cách nhau ba mươi dặm trên cùng một đoạn bờ biển. Cả hai từng là cảng cá cho đến những năm 1980, khi ngành này sụp đổ. Cả hai mất một nửa dân số trong thập kỷ tiếp theo, và cả hai chuyển sang du lịch để tồn tại. Hôm nay, một thị trấn đang phát triển. Thị trấn còn lại đang bị chính thành công của mình ăn tươi nuốt sống. Thị trấn đầu tiên, tạm gọi là Greycombe, may mắn vì những người dẫn dắt sự hồi phục. Các uỷ viên hội đồng địa phương quyết định rằng du lịch chỉ có ích nếu tạo ra việc làm quanh năm và bảo vệ những gì làm thị trấn đáng đến thăm ngay từ đầu. Họ giới hạn khách sạn mới chỉ được xây dọc bờ biển, từ chối việc chuyển đổi nhà sang cho thuê ngắn hạn ở khu trung tâm cũ, và đầu tư vào một chương trình mùa đông nhỏ nhưng đều đặn gồm các lễ hội và chợ thực phẩm. Kết quả là dân số đã ổn định, trường học vẫn mở, và phần lớn nhà cửa vẫn do người thực sự sống ở đó sở hữu. Thị trấn thứ hai, tạm gọi là Marshpoint, đi theo cách ngược lại. Bất cứ ai muốn chuyển một căn nhà ngư dân thành chỗ cho thuê du lịch ngắn hạn đều được chào đón. Trong mười năm, hơn một phần ba khu trung tâm cũ đã bị mua hết bởi những người chỉ ghé thăm ba tuần mỗi năm. Người dân lớn lên ở đó không mua nổi nhà, và nhiều người đã chuyển vào sâu trong đất liền. Phố chính đầy cửa hàng kẹo fudge và cho thuê ván lướt, nhưng tiệm bánh đã đóng, hiệu thuốc đã đóng, và trường tiểu học đang vật lộn vì thiếu học sinh. Khi bạn đi quanh Marshpoint vào tháng Hai, cảm giác như một phông cảnh sân khấu sau khi vở diễn đã kết thúc. Điều này cho ta thấy không phải du lịch là xấu. Greycombe đã chứng minh rằng cùng một ngành có thể nuôi sống một cộng đồng thực. Sự khác biệt nằm ở hai câu hỏi mà gần như không thị trấn nào đặt ra đủ sớm. Thứ nhất là: chúng ta muốn trở thành thị trấn thế nào trong hai mươi năm tới? Thứ hai là: ai thực sự sống ở đây, và ai chỉ ở đây cuối tuần? Nếu câu trả lời câu thứ hai thành 'chủ yếu là người cuối tuần', thì câu trả lời câu thứ nhất đang được quyết định bởi những người không gánh chịu chi phí. Đến lúc một thị trấn nhận ra điều này, chi phí để đảo ngược thường cao hơn chi phí để ngăn ngừa. Không có quy tắc hoàn hảo nào để quản lý du lịch, và sao chép Greycombe sẽ không cứu được Marshpoint. Điều kiện kinh tế khác, quyền quy hoạch khác, và ý chí chính trị khác. Nhưng bài học rộng vẫn đúng: một thị trấn để du lịch thay thế các ngành công nghiệp khác cuối cùng sẽ trở thành bảo tàng của chính nó, được duy trì bởi những du khách sẽ mất hứng thú nếu người địa phương biến mất hoàn toàn. Du lịch có thể cứu một thị trấn. Nó cũng có thể làm rỗng thị trấn đó âm thầm đến mức không ai nhận ra cho đến khi trường học đóng cửa. Sự khác biệt nằm ở các quy tắc mà thị trấn đặt ra khi nó vẫn còn quyền đặt ra chúng.

questions.prompt: How far apart are the two towns?
questions.explanation: The narrator says they 'sit thirty miles apart on the same stretch of coast.'
questions.hint: A specific distance is given at the start.

questions.prompt: What was the original industry of both towns?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Both were fishing ports until the 1980s.'

questions.prompt: What three policies did Greycombe's councillors put in place?
options: Free parking, free Wi-Fi, free festivals
Hotels limited to the seafront, no short-term rental conversions in the old centre, a winter festival programme
Higher taxes, longer working hours, more advertising
Bigger cruise terminals, longer beaches, faster trains
questions.explanation: The narrator lists three policies: 'limited new hotels to the seafront, refused short-term rental conversions in the old centre, and invested in a small but reliable winter programme.'

questions.prompt: What is the result in Greycombe today?
options: The population has stabilised, schools have stayed open, and most houses are occupied by residents
It has been bought by a single hotel chain
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'the population has stabilised, schools have stayed open, and most of the houses are still occupied by people who actually live there.'

options: It welcomed anyone who wanted to convert a cottage into a short-term let
It built only large hotels on the cliff
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Anyone who wanted to convert a fisherman's cottage into a short-term holiday let was welcomed.'
questions.hint: Marshpoint's policy was permissive, not restrictive.

questions.prompt: What proportion of the old centre was bought up in ten years?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'more than a third of the old centre had been bought up.'

questions.prompt: Why have local people moved inland from Marshpoint?
options: Because they cannot afford to buy a house there
Because of pollution from cruise ships
Because the council forced them out
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Locals who grew up there cannot afford to buy a house, and many have moved inland.'

questions.prompt: What has closed in Marshpoint's high street?
options: The bakery, the chemist, and the school is struggling
The library and the post office
The hospital and the bus station
Nothing — everything is full of tourists
questions.explanation: The narrator lists 'the bakery has closed, the chemist has closed, and the primary school is struggling for pupils.'

questions.prompt: How does Marshpoint feel in February?
options: Like a stage set after the play has ended
Like a quiet but ordinary residential area
questions.explanation: The narrator says it 'feels like a stage set after the play has ended.'
questions.hint: The image is theatrical and empty.

questions.prompt: What two questions does the narrator say towns rarely ask early enough?
options: How much will tourists spend, and how can we attract more?
What kind of town do we want to be in twenty years, and who actually lives here?
Where will tourists park, and where will they eat?
Who pays the taxes, and who pays the bills?
questions.explanation: The narrator identifies the two questions: 'what kind of town do we want to be in twenty years' and 'who actually lives here, and who is just here for the weekend?'
questions.hint: Two long-term identity questions, not commercial ones.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say about copying Greycombe's model?
options: It will not save Marshpoint because economic conditions, planning powers, and political will are different
It is illegal under national law
It is the only way forward
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'copying Greycombe will not save Marshpoint. The economic conditions are different, the planning powers are different, and the political will is different.'
questions.hint: The narrator rejects simple copying with three reasons.

questions.prompt: What is the broad lesson the narrator draws?
options: A town that lets tourism replace its other industries will eventually become a museum of itself
Tourism can replace all other industries safely
The economic value of tourism outweighs all costs
questions.explanation: The narrator says a town 'that lets tourism replace its other industries will eventually become a museum of itself.'
questions.hint: The lesson is about replacement, not tourism itself.

questions.prompt: What is the central comparison structuring the passage?
options: Two towns that took opposite policies toward tourism after losing the same industry
Two seasons in the same town
questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly contrasts Greycombe and Marshpoint as two towns starting from the same point but ending in different places.
questions.hint: Look at the structural pairing of 'first town' and 'second town'.

cloze.template: Two small towns sit thirty miles apart on the same stretch of coast. Both were fishing {{b1}} until the 1980s, when the industry collapsed. Both turned to tourism to survive. Today, one is thriving. The other is being eaten alive by its own {{b2}}. Greycombe was lucky in the people who led the recovery. They limited new hotels to the seafront, refused short-term rental {{b3}} in the old centre, and invested in a small winter programme. The population has {{b4}}, schools have stayed open, and most of the houses are still occupied by people who actually live there. Marshpoint took the opposite approach. Within ten years, more than a third of the old centre had been {{b5}} up by people who only visit for three weeks a year. Locals cannot afford to buy a house and many have moved {{b6}}. The bakery has closed, the chemist has closed, and the primary school is struggling for {{b7}}. The difference lies in two questions almost no town asks early enough. What kind of town do we want to be in twenty years? And who actually lives here, and who is just here for the {{b8}}? A town that lets tourism replace its other industries will eventually become a {{b9}} of itself. The difference is in the rules a town sets while it still has the power to {{b10}} them.

blanks.explanation: 'Fishing ports' is the exact phrase used — a port is the standard term for a coastal town with a working harbour.
'Eaten alive by its own success' is the exact idiomatic phrase the narrator uses.
'Short-term rental conversions' is the exact phrase — the conversion is what the council blocked.
'Stabilised' is the exact verb — meaning it stopped declining and held steady.
'Bought up' is the exact phrasal verb the narrator uses for large-scale purchase.
'Moved inland' is the exact directional phrase — away from the coast.
'Pupils' is the standard British term for primary-school children — the exact word used.
'Here for the weekend' is the exact phrase contrasting weekenders with permanent residents.
'Museum of itself' is the narrator's exact image — preserved appearance without living function.
'Set the rules' is the fixed collocation — the same verb used twice in the sentence for parallelism.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says the cost of reversing tourist-led decline is usually higher than the cost of preventing it. Why do communities so often act too late, even when the warning signs are visible — and is it ever possible to set rules that survive a change of council?
